intro: Five tasks against the real androidx.lifecycle.ViewModel APIs: state exposed as a StateFlow and mutated with update {}, work in viewModelScope that stops for good when the ViewModel is cleared, a SavedStateHandle-backed value that survives a simulated process death, a ViewModelProvider.Factory that injects a constructor dependency, and a repository Flow turned into shared UI state with stateIn. Each task has a failing test to turn green, and the tutor walks you through each one and ticks it off as your tests pass.

tasks.title: CounterViewModel - StateFlow mutated via update {}
tasks.detail: Implement increment and reset on CounterViewModel so they mutate the private MutableStateFlow through update {}, never by reassigning .value directly.
tasks.hint: _count.update { it + 1 } for increment, _count.update { 0 } for reset. update {} is the safe, atomic way to mutate a MutableStateFlow from any caller.
tasks.verify: T1CounterViewModelTest is green: count starts at 0, three increments land on 3, and reset brings it back to 0.

tasks.title: TickerViewModel.startTicking - work that dies with the ViewModel
tasks.detail: Launch a coroutine in viewModelScope that increments tickCount once a second, forever. Because viewModelScope is cancelled automatically right before onCleared runs, this loop needs to actually stop producing ticks once the ViewModel is cleared, not just get ignored.
tasks.hint: viewModelScope.launch { while (true) { delay(1000); _tickCount.update { it + 1 } } }. The test proves cancellation by building the ViewModel through a real ViewModelStore/ViewModelProvider, then calling store.clear() and checking tickCount stops moving.
tasks.verify: T2TickerViewModelTest is green: tickCount climbs by 1 per second while the ViewModel is alive, and freezes exactly where it was once the owning ViewModelStore is cleared.

tasks.title: NoteViewModel - a value that survives process death
tasks.detail: Back noteState with savedStateHandle.getStateFlow(KEY_NOTE, "") so it starts from whatever was already saved, and make updateNote write through the same handle with savedStateHandle[KEY_NOTE] = text so a ViewModel rebuilt with that handle sees the latest value.
tasks.hint: val noteState: StateFlow<String> = savedStateHandle.getStateFlow(KEY_NOTE, "") as the property initializer; updateNote just does savedStateHandle[KEY_NOTE] = text.
tasks.verify: T3NoteViewModelTest is green: noteState starts empty, updateNote is reflected immediately, and a second NoteViewModel built with the same SavedStateHandle (simulating process death) sees the restored note.

tasks.title: GreeterViewModelFactory.create - inject a constructor dependency
tasks.detail: Implement create() so it builds a GreeterViewModel using the greeting this factory was constructed with, instead of relying on GreeterViewModel having a no-arg constructor.
tasks.hint: @Suppress("UNCHECKED_CAST") return GreeterViewModel(greeting) as T. This is the whole point of a custom Factory: it lets a ViewModel take constructor arguments that ViewModelProvider itself can't guess.
tasks.verify: T4GreeterViewModelFactoryTest is green: the greeting passed into the factory ends up on the built ViewModel, the provider caches and reuses that instance, and two factories with different greetings produce independently-configured ViewModels.

tasks.title: ItemsViewModel.items - stateIn with WhileSubscribed(5000)
tasks.detail: Turn repository.items into items using repository.items.stateIn(viewModelScope, SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000), emptyList()), so every collector shares one upstream subscription that starts on the first subscriber and is kept alive for 5 seconds after the last one leaves.
tasks.hint: repository.items.stateIn(viewModelScope, SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000), emptyList()) is the whole implementation. The 5 second grace period is what stops a quick screen rotation from retriggering the repository's fetch.
tasks.verify: T5ItemsViewModelTest is green: the repository isn't touched until the first collector subscribes, a brief gap between collectors reuses the same upstream subscription, and only once the 5 second grace window fully elapses does the next collector trigger a fresh fetch.
